reflection: ITER-0004c started as a 6-step scope claiming a single iteration could land STORY-0010 entirely. PAR over 4 rounds caught: (1) ast_to_ir_indexed.fmpl was silently boxed-out and would break AC-13 grep gate; (2) ~50 Rust test files use :Tag(args) source strings, no AC covered them; (3) SCENARIO-0103 only covers Phase A, leaving the same regression mode that swallowed ITER-0004b's Phase C; (4) AC-8/AC-15 already shipped by ITER-0004b, double-counted; (5) scope item 4 referenced nonexistent compile_and_load(); (6) bootstrap loader needed 'let ast_optimizer = io::load(...)' wrap because ast_optimizer.fmpl ends with bare map literal; (7) pipeline_demo.fmpl:9 string literal triggers verification grep gate; (8) AC-13 mis-assigned to Phase B but actually delivered by Phase A's stdlib migration; (9) ac3_int_min_negation_does_not_panic test uses unparseable source 9223372036854775808 because lexer parse::<i64>().ok() drops it. The split into ITER-0004c (stdlib + optimizer) + ITER-0004d (parser/AST burn) was the structural fix. PAR caught real defects each round; going from R1 to R4 the findings became more granular and implementation-specific, suggesting additional rounds would yield diminishing returns. Lesson: PAR is genuinely valuable for catching boxing-in and citation-drift in iteration plans — never skip it on iterations that span >1 file or >1 system.

detail: 3 REVISE verdicts; split iteration into 3; surfaced planning miss (AC-14 already satisfied) and dead-on-arrival rename map; checkpointed for fresh-session resume
reflection: PAR rounds 1-3 each surfaced more findings (15 → 23 → 30+) because each layer of roadmap polish exposed the next layer of dependent-site enumeration the prior round didn't reach. Round 3 in particular caught two structural errors that no amount of text polish could fix: (1) AC-14 was already satisfied by ITER-0004b (Value::Tagged literals absent from tests; verified via grep). The 'AC-14 sweep' I was planning was actually a new project invariant (FMPL :Tag(args) strings inside Rust test source) conflated with AC-14 by both me and the original roadmap author. (2) ITER-0004d.2's rename map for tagged bytecode opcodes was dead-on-arrival — ITER-0004d.1's consumer-arm deletions remove ALL the compiler.rs emit sites listed for renaming. Real surviving sites are in ir_builder.rs, builtins/ir.rs, builtins/ir_to_rust.rs:543 (missed entirely). Lesson for future iterations: when planning a refactor that deletes producer arms AND renames opcodes, the rename-target enumeration must come AFTER a cargo-check-driven view of what survives deletion, not before. PAR can catch citation drift but cannot enumerate sites the planner didn't think to grep for. The 'build the tooling precursor first, then let cargo errors enumerate the next layer' approach is the cheaper alternative to N polish rounds.

reflection: Built per active hypothesis after 3 prior PAR rounds on monolithic ITER-0004d went speculative. User direction was 'build as-spec'd; surface real data' rather than another revision cycle. Two design contradictions were hit and resolved during implementation: (1) syn cannot be [dev-dependencies] AND used from src/diagnostics — resolved by splitting scan_fmpl_source (src, syn-free) from scan_rust_strings (tests/common, dev-syn); (2) lexer-level Symbol+LParen detector produces grammar-DSL false positives (unary:first ( ... ) tokenizes identically to a tagged-constructor) — resolved via allowlist of 6 known sites. Post-impl PAR caught 2 inline-fixable bugs (lying rust_byte_offset=0 → Option<usize>; operator-symbol filtering). Deeper concerns (macro bodies, walker asymmetry, strict-equality baseline, allowlist coarseness) deferred to d.1 with documentation. Lesson: when speculation has failed twice in PAR, shipping a tool that produces concrete numbers beats one more revision round.
